_meta.note: Single content source for portfolio-shwetabh.lexnovahq.com. Stripped/watermarked-safe: no commercial framing, no Architect Notes, no secrets. Matrix rows derived from verified LEX_NOVA_REGISTRY_v2 (98 threats).
_meta.page_title: Shwetabh Singh — Technology & Data Protection Lawyer building legal delivery systems
_meta.og_title: Shwetabh Singh — Technology & Data Protection Lawyer building legal delivery systems
_meta.og_description: AI governance, cross-border privacy, commercial contracts, and the legal delivery systems that make the work structured, reviewable, and deployable.

hero.name: Shwetabh Singh
hero.identity_line: Technology & Data Protection Lawyer | AI Governance · Cross-Border Privacy · Commercial Contracts for Tech
hero.builder_line: Building legal delivery systems that make AI governance, privacy, and contract work structured, reviewable, and deployable.
hero.spine_explainer: The path the work actually took — law first, systems second.
hero.hook: My legal foundation is 3.5 years of litigation. Since October 2025, I have been turning AI governance and commercial-contract work into structured legal systems — risk registries, diligence workflows, client intake logic, document assembly, and review portals — so the work becomes easier to review, repeat, and deploy.
hero.thesis: The hard part of legal AI isn't the AI — it's making a probabilistic machine deterministic enough to trust with law. That's what I engineered.

proof_chips.label: AI Risk Diligence Engine
proof_chips.what: A diligence layer that turns public/product facts into legal-risk findings using a structured threat registry.
proof_chips.supports: Supports AI governance review by showing what risk is triggered, why it matters, and what legal instrument should respond.
proof_chips.proof: Shown through project walkthrough and scrubbed proof artifacts.

proof_chips.label: Internal AI Use Review
proof_chips.what: A review workflow for shadow AI, employee tool use, client-data exposure, output ownership, and workplace governance.
proof_chips.supports: Supports internal AI policy, handbook, impact assessment, IP, and human-review controls.
proof_chips.proof: Shown through the workplace governance scanner and related document stack.

proof_chips.label: Client Product Profile Vault
proof_chips.what: A structured intake vault that maps product facts — market exposure, model architecture, output ownership, SLA posture, PII processing, and AI archetype — into document-routing logic.
proof_chips.supports: Supports ToS, DPA, AUP, SLA, Agentic Addendum, Privacy Policy, and related review-ready documents.
proof_chips.proof: Built as a four-module intake wizard with clause-injection mappings.

proof_chips.label: Risk-to-Clause Mapping System
proof_chips.what: A legal mapping layer that connects risks, authorities, trigger conditions, and remediation clauses.
proof_chips.supports: Supports reviewability: a reviewer can trace why a clause exists and what legal risk it closes.
proof_chips.proof: Shown through the threat matrix and living document interaction.

proof_chips.label: Document Assembly Workflow
proof_chips.what: A workflow for turning client/product inputs into a structured document stack instead of hand-building every draft from zero.
proof_chips.supports: Supports repeatable legal delivery while keeping human review and legal judgment in the loop.
proof_chips.proof: Shown through the Vault-to-document assembly path.

proof_chips.label: Client Review Portal
proof_chips.what: A delivery layer for client intake, review, document status, and structured handoff.
proof_chips.supports: Supports review-ready delivery: the legal work is organized for client and counsel review.
proof_chips.proof: Shown through safe/scrubbed portal proof artifacts.

hero.proof_line: Demos and videos use public, synthetic, or scrubbed proof artifacts only.
hero.credential_bar: 3.5 years litigation · B.A. LL.B., NLU Odisha · AI governance · privacy · commercial contracts · building hands-on since Oct 2025

nodes.label: Start
nodes.why: Establishes the claim: lawyer first, systems second.
Shows the legal source layer: cases, statutes, and risk principles.
Shows how AI use cases become legal risk categories.
Shows how mapped risks become clauses, policies, and playbooks.
Shows the diligence and intake workflows that operationalize the legal work.
Shows the reusable delivery layer: portal, vault, and automation architecture.

nodes.label: Close
nodes.why: Converts the proof into the hiring argument for legal tech practice.

act1.orientation_line: This portfolio runs in two halves. Acts I–II are the law — every AI risk mapped, and the clauses that close it. Act III is the systems I built to do that work automatically. The systems exist to deliver the law.
act1.header: The Legal Foundation — AI law risks mapped into a working taxonomy.
act1.forward_ref: → what the diligence engine later detects on its own.
act1.intro: Each one stays close to the real legal problem — what creates the exposure, what closes it, what holds up. I built them in AI law: contracts, compliance, privacy, AI liability, diligence, digital business risk. The build is just the delivery — the legal judgment is the point.

reference_layer.frame: The reference layer the systems read from.
reference_layer.worked_line: A model is probabilistic (mechanism) → its confident false output creates reliance liability (consequence) → a hallucination-specific waiver + output-authority boundary closes it (remediation).
reference_layer.scope_stat: 98 threats · 10 archetypes · 10 surfaces · 3 jurisdictions — authored solo since October 2025, refined from 81 through forensic-engine runs.
reference_layer.applied_practice: Applied-practice layer shown frosted, with a count only. No raw corpus, no commercial labels.

risk_map.note: Classify → trigger. Select an archetype → its surfaces light over the Global Floor → click a lit surface → modal pulls the matching registry rows (authority IN/EU/US + clause). Preset-driven entry; on-hover definitions on every archetype and surface.

global_floor.label: Global Floor (Universal)
global_floor.def: The baseline every AI product is bound by, regardless of what it does — consent, transparency, third-party pass-throughs.

archetypes.name: Doer
archetypes.def: Executes tasks or spends money autonomously (AI SDRs, automated support, agents).

archetypes.name: Judge
archetypes.def: Scores or screens humans for opportunity (HR screeners, loan approvals).

archetypes.name: Companion
archetypes.def: Sustains emotional relationships or roleplay (virtual therapists, conversational agents).

archetypes.name: Creator
archetypes.def: Generates net-new media or code (image generators, voice cloners, copilots).

archetypes.name: Reader
archetypes.def: Consumes and vectorizes external data (RAG pipelines, scrapers).

archetypes.name: Orchestrator
archetypes.def: Routes prompts to third-party models (API gateways, model routers).

archetypes.name: Translator
archetypes.def: Bridges formats, often via physical inputs (meeting note-takers, speech-to-text).

archetypes.name: Shield
archetypes.def: Monitors or audits enterprise environments (AI pen-testing, compliance bots).

archetypes.name: Optimizer
archetypes.def: Manages critical or financial infrastructure (algorithmic trading, grid control).

archetypes.name: Mover
archetypes.def: Acts on the physical world (warehouse robotics, autonomous drones).

surfaces.name: PII
surfaces.def: Processing identifiable personal data — names, emails, IPs, user text.

surfaces.name: Sensitive/Biometric
surfaces.def: Health, voice, or facial-geometry data — triggers strict biometric regimes.

surfaces.name: Financial
surfaces.def: AI managing trades, credit, or payments.

surfaces.name: Minors
surfaces.def: Users under 18 — triggers age-design and parental-consent regimes.

surfaces.name: Employment
surfaces.def: Screening candidates or employees — triggers bias-audit mandates.

surfaces.name: Consumer-Public
surfaces.def: Mass-market B2C exposure — dark-patterns, takedown, class-action surface.

surfaces.name: Enterprise-Private
surfaces.def: B2B SaaS to large organizations — SLA and wasted-expenditure exposure.

surfaces.name: Infrastructure
surfaces.def: Reliance on foundation-model APIs or critical infrastructure.

surfaces.name: Content&IP
surfaces.def: Generated media, training data, and ownership of output.

presets.label: Customer chatbot
presets.description: Conversational AI answering customers or users.

presets.label: AI support agent
presets.description: AI that responds to tickets, handles customer support, or recommends actions.

presets.label: Hiring / screening tool
presets.description: AI that screens, ranks, scores, or recommends candidates or employees.

presets.label: Coding copilot
presets.description: AI that generates, reviews, or assists with code.

presets.label: Meeting note-taker
presets.description: AI that records, transcribes, summarizes, or analyzes meetings.

presets.label: AI SDR / outbound agent
presets.description: AI that sends, sequences, personalizes, or triggers sales/outbound actions.

presets.label: RAG knowledge bot
presets.description: AI that ingests documents and retrieves answers from a knowledge base.

presets.label: Trading / payment agent
presets.description: AI that acts on financial signals, payment flows, accounts, or transaction logic.

risk_map.diagnostic_format: Mechanical trigger → Law (IN / US / EU) → Remediation

law.eu: GDPR Art. 22 + AI Act Annex III (high-risk; 2 Dec 2027)
law.us: Title VII / EEOC · NYC LL144 · IL HB3773 · TX TRAIGA

risk_map.title: The Risk Map — classify the AI, then identify the legal surface.
risk_map.intro: The map works in two moves. First, classify what the AI does. Second, identify what legal surface it touches. The output is a triggered risk with authority and remediation.
risk_map.move1_heading: Move 1 — Classify what the AI does
risk_map.move1_explanation: The first question is functional, not promotional. Ignore the company’s marketing label. Ask what the AI actually does.
risk_map.preset_instruction: Select a common AI use case to light the underlying AI function.
risk_map.move2_heading: Move 2 — Identify what legal surface it touches
risk_map.move2_explanation: The same AI function can trigger different legal risks depending on what it touches: personal data, employment, IP, financial decisions, consumers, infrastructure, or physical safety.
risk_map.output_explanation: Once function and surface combine, the system can identify the legal risk, cite the authority, and route the remediation into the right instrument or workflow.
risk_map.surface_instruction: Click a lit surface to see why the risk is triggered.

case_law.name: Moffatt v. Air Canada
case_law.line: The chatbot speaks with the company's authority; no “separate tool” defence.

case_law.name: Thaler v. Perlmutter
case_law.line: No copyright for autonomous AI output; forces human-in-the-loop.

case_law.name: Bartz v. Anthropic
case_law.line: The training-data reckoning; forces upstream IP indemnities.

case_law.name: Mobley v. Workday
case_law.line: AI hiring tools face disparate-impact liability.

case_law.name: FTC v. Rite Aid
case_law.line: Algorithmic disgorgement; a regulator can force the model destroyed.

case_law.name: Cruz v. Fireflies
case_law.line: Meeting-capture AI triggers strict biometric / recording consent.

act1.connector1: A taxonomy protects no one on its own. Once the risk can be classified, it must be routed into clauses, policies, playbooks, and review-ready instruments. That is Act II.
act1.orientation_purpose: The legal work comes first. The systems exist only to deliver it faster, more consistently, and in a reviewable form.
act1.subline: Before I built the tools, I built the legal source layer they would read from: AI physics, legal doctrine, defensive controls, and product/instrument routing.
act1.what_this_proves: This section shows how technical AI behavior becomes legal risk, how legal risk becomes a control, and how that control is routed into a document, workflow, or system.

first_fork.title: First Fork — where does the AI risk live?
first_fork.subtitle: Before the taxonomy, the work splits by legal context. The question is not only what does the AI do? The first question is: is the company building AI for others, or using AI inside its own business?

lanes.who: Companies building, selling, or exposing AI products to customers, users, APIs, or enterprise buyers.
lanes.explanation: Lane A asks: if the AI product fails, misleads, processes data, generates output, or acts for a user, what legal architecture protects the company?

lanes.who: Companies using AI inside the business through employees, contractors, teams, departments, or operational workflows.
lanes.explanation: Lane B asks: if employees or teams use AI inside the business, how do we control data, authorship, confidentiality, reliance, and human review?

first_fork.transition: Once the lane is clear, the analysis moves into the source layer: what the AI technically does, what legal doctrine that triggers, what defensive control responds, and where the fix must live.

ai_bible.title: The AI Bible — the source layer behind the taxonomy
ai_bible.intro: The taxonomy starts from AI behavior, not document templates. The source layer asks four questions: what the system technically does, what legal doctrine that triggers, what control contains the risk, and which instrument or workflow carries the fix.

columns.title: AI physics
columns.text: What the system technically does: generate, infer, remember, route, act, score, ingest, or automate.

columns.title: Legal doctrine
columns.text: The legal exposure triggered by that behavior: reliance, authority, privacy, authorship, consent, discrimination, deletion, or product/service classification.

columns.title: Defensive control
columns.text: The legal architecture that contains the risk: waiver, boundary, audit, consent gate, human review, cap, pass-through, or prohibition.

columns.title: Product / instrument routing
columns.text: Where the control lives: ToS, DPA, AUP, SLA, Agentic Addendum, Handbook, IP Deed, SOP, DPIA, Vault intake, diligence engine, or portal workflow.

rows.title: Probabilistic Output
rows.ai_physics: Probabilistic output can be confidently wrong.
rows.legal_doctrine: Reliance, chatbot authority, warranty, misrepresentation, and customer-facing output risk.
rows.defensive_control: Hallucination waiver, output authority boundary, point-of-interaction warning, and human verification trigger.
rows.routing: ToS · AUP · diligence engine · product UI warning
rows.why: Generic AI may be wrong language is not enough. The control must sit where reliance happens and must define what the AI output is legally allowed to be.

rows.title: Agentic Action
rows.ai_physics: Agentic systems can act, retry, spend, route, trigger workflows, or bind a process without a fresh human click.
rows.legal_doctrine: Electronic agent attribution, agency risk, authority, automated transaction risk, and operational liability.
rows.defensive_control: Spend caps, retry limits, loop thresholds, circuit breakers, approval gates, audit logs, and authority boundaries.
rows.routing: Agentic Addendum · ToS · governance log · Vault intake
rows.why: Once AI moves from answering to acting, the legal problem changes from wrong output to who is responsible for machine action?

rows.title: AI Memory / Fine-Tuning
rows.ai_physics: Fine-tuned or training-based memory is not cleanly deletable like a database row.
rows.legal_doctrine: Erasure rights, privacy compliance, model disgorgement, data retention, and subprocessor transparency.
rows.defensive_control: RAG-only mandate, deletion architecture, training prohibition, subprocessor disclosure, and data-use boundary.
rows.routing: DPA · Privacy Policy · Vault intake · DPIA
rows.why: If user data becomes part of model weights, deletion and downstream control become much harder. The legal architecture must be designed around the technical memory model.

rows.title: AI Output / Authorship
rows.ai_physics: Autonomous AI output may not carry human authorship by default.
rows.legal_doctrine: Copyright ownership, assignment failure, authorship evidence, work-product control, and IP-chain risk.
rows.defensive_control: Human-in-the-loop authorship protocol, prompt/work-product assignment, contributor warranty, authorship evidence trail.
rows.routing: IP Deed · HITL SOP · ToS output terms · Workplace Handbook
rows.why: If the business cannot prove a human authorship chain, we own the output may be commercially weak even if the document says it.

rows.title: AI Scoring / Decisioning
rows.ai_physics: AI systems can score, rank, screen, recommend, or influence access to opportunity.
rows.legal_doctrine: Bias, discrimination, automated decisioning, employment screening, high-risk decisioning, and explainability risk.
rows.defensive_control: Bias audit, human review, prohibited-use boundary, appeal/escalation route, and impact assessment.
rows.routing: DPIA · AUP · Handbook · HITL SOP · diligence engine
rows.why: The legal issue is not only the model’s output. It is whether the output affects a person’s opportunity, rights, employment, credit, access, or treatment.

rows.title: Consent / Online Terms
rows.ai_physics: AI products often operate through fast online flows where terms, warnings, notices, and consents are buried or skipped.
rows.legal_doctrine: Clickwrap enforceability, assent, notice, consent validity, dark-pattern risk, and consumer protection.
rows.defensive_control: Affirmative consent UI, visible notice architecture, acceptance logs, renewal/cancellation clarity, and point-of-action warning.
rows.routing: ToS · Privacy Policy · UI mandate · product workflow · portal records
rows.why: A strong clause is useless if the user never validly assented to it. Legal architecture must reach the interface layer.

taxonomy_bridge.title: From source layer to working taxonomy
taxonomy_bridge.intro: The AI Bible is the reasoning layer. The taxonomy turns that reasoning into units a diligence engine can read.
taxonomy_bridge.scope_stat: 98 threat units · 10 AI functions · 10 legal surfaces · 3 jurisdiction bands
taxonomy_bridge.explanation: Each threat unit is structured so the system can ask: what AI behavior is present, what legal surface is touched, what authority is relevant, what consequence follows, and what instrument should respond.
taxonomy_bridge.confidentiality_note: The full source layer and applied-practice notes are not exposed here. The visible layer shows the reasoning architecture, selected examples, and the taxonomy logic.

worked_examples.default: Select a common AI use case above. The example below will show how the taxonomy turns an AI use case into a legal-risk route.

customer-chatbot.move1: Companion — the AI speaks directly to users in a conversational interface.
customer-chatbot.output: Reliance, misleading output, customer-facing disclosure, privacy, and consent risk.
customer-chatbot.authority: Moffatt v. Air Canada · Walters v. OpenAI · privacy notice / consent principles
customer-chatbot.remediation: Hallucination waiver, output authority boundary, point-of-interaction warning, privacy notice, and escalation path.
customer-chatbot.routes_into: ToS · Privacy Policy · AUP · diligence engine

ai-support-agent.move1: Doer / Companion — the AI answers customers and may recommend or trigger support actions.
ai-support-agent.output: Wrong support advice, escalation failure, data exposure, and automated action risk.
ai-support-agent.authority: Chatbot authority and reliance principles · data protection notice/processing duties · electronic action risk where workflows are triggered
ai-support-agent.remediation: Human escalation route, support-output disclaimer, authority boundary, processing disclosure, and action limits.
ai-support-agent.routes_into: ToS · Privacy Policy · AUP · support workflow · portal records

hiring-screening-tool.move1: Judge — the AI scores, ranks, screens, or recommends humans.
hiring-screening-tool.output: Bias, disparate impact, automated decisioning, explainability, and employment-data risk.
hiring-screening-tool.authority: GDPR Art. 22 logic · EU AI Act high-risk employment logic · employment discrimination principles · workplace privacy principles
hiring-screening-tool.remediation: Bias audit, human-in-the-loop review, use restriction, audit log, appeal/escalation path, and impact assessment.
hiring-screening-tool.routes_into: DPIA · AUP · AI Use Handbook · HITL SOP

coding-copilot.move1: Creator — the AI generates or materially assists with code.
coding-copilot.output: Ownership uncertainty, open-source contamination, security reliance, and human authorship risk.
coding-copilot.authority: Thaler v. Perlmutter · IP ownership / assignment principles · software warranty and reliance risk
coding-copilot.remediation: HITL authorship protocol, output review, IP assignment boundary, open-source review, and prohibited reliance rule.
coding-copilot.routes_into: IP Deed · HITL SOP · ToS output terms · AUP

meeting-note-taker.move1: Translator / Reader — the AI records, transcribes, summarizes, or analyzes spoken content.
meeting-note-taker.output: Recording consent, biometric/voice risk, confidential information leakage, and privacy disclosure risk.
meeting-note-taker.authority: Cruz v. Fireflies · privacy notice/consent principles · confidentiality and workplace privacy principles
meeting-note-taker.remediation: meeting consent notice, recording disclosure, retention limit, processing disclosure, and confidential-data handling rule.
meeting-note-taker.routes_into: Privacy Policy · DPA · AI Use Handbook · DPIA

ai-sdr-outbound-agent.move1: Doer / Orchestrator — the AI personalizes, sequences, sends, routes, or triggers outbound actions.
ai-sdr-outbound-agent.output: automated outreach, consent, spam/dark-pattern, data processing, and action-authority risk.
ai-sdr-outbound-agent.authority: consent and marketing law principles · electronic agent/action attribution principles · consumer protection principles
ai-sdr-outbound-agent.remediation: audience limits, consent/opt-out routing, human approval for campaigns, authority boundary, and audit logs.
ai-sdr-outbound-agent.routes_into: AUP · ToS · Privacy Policy · Agentic Addendum · outbound workflow

rag-knowledge-bot.move1: Reader — the AI ingests, indexes, retrieves, and answers from documents or knowledge bases.
rag-knowledge-bot.output: data ingestion, retention, retrieval leakage, source provenance, and deletion risk.
rag-knowledge-bot.authority: FTC v. Rite Aid logic · GDPR/DPDP deletion and processing principles · confidentiality and subprocessor transparency principles
rag-knowledge-bot.remediation: RAG-only mandate, source logging, access controls, deletion architecture, and subprocessor disclosure.
rag-knowledge-bot.routes_into: DPA · Privacy Policy · Vault intake · DPIA · security workflow

trading-payment-agent.move1: Optimizer / Doer — the AI manages or triggers financial decisions, payment flows, or transaction actions.
trading-payment-agent.output: financial reliance, unauthorized transaction, automation error, and high-stakes decision risk.
trading-payment-agent.authority: electronic agent/action attribution principles · financial reliance and consumer protection principles · service classification risk
trading-payment-agent.remediation: transaction caps, human approval threshold, prohibited reliance warning, audit logs, and circuit breaker.
trading-payment-agent.routes_into: Agentic Addendum · ToS · AUP · governance log

source_rule_spine.title: Source Rule Spine — authority converted into operating rules
source_rule_spine.intro: The cases are not citations for decoration. Each source creates a rule that becomes a registry condition, drafting control, or workflow requirement.

rows.rule: Chatbot output can bind the company where users reasonably rely on it.
rows.powers: Output authority boundary · hallucination controls · customer-facing warning design

rows.rule: Point-of-interaction disclaimers matter because reliance forms at the moment of use.
rows.powers: interface warning · output disclaimer · reliance limitation

rows.rule: Autonomous AI output may fail human authorship requirements.
rows.powers: HITL authorship protocol · IP assignment structure · work-product evidence trail

rows.rule: Training-data provenance can become a major copyright and dataset-risk issue.
rows.powers: dataset provenance controls · upstream IP indemnity · training-data review

rows.rule: Regulators can require deletion or destruction of tainted algorithmic systems.
rows.powers: RAG-only mandate · deletion architecture · model-memory restrictions

rows.source: Specht v. Netscape / Meyer v. Uber
rows.rule: Online terms must be visible, affirmative, and reasonably presented to bind users.
rows.powers: clickwrap design · consent logs · UI mandate · notice architecture

rows.rule: Contract structure can affect whether software access is treated as a license rather than ownership transfer.
rows.powers: service/access classification · license boundary · platform-use restrictions

rows.rule: Liability wording can preserve or exclude wasted expenditure and major commercial loss categories.
rows.powers: liability cap architecture · exclusion drafting · enterprise negotiation playbook

act1.connector_visual: Risk classified → control selected → instrument routed.

act2.header: The Legal Matrix — threat IDs converted into clauses, policies, playbooks, and review-ready instruments.
act2.subline: Act I classified the risk. Act II shows how that risk becomes legal architecture: a threat ID, an authority, a defensive control, and the instrument that carries the fix.
act2.core_visual_line: Risk classified → control selected → instrument routed
act2.explanation: The registry is not a list of legal issues. It is a routing layer: each threat ID carries the risk, authority, control logic, and document destination.
act2.guide_line: Follow the route: matrix first, instrument map second, clause viewer third.

threat_matrix.title: Threat Matrix — the registry behind the instruments
threat_matrix.intro: The threat matrix converts AI-law risk into structured rows: threat ID, lane, AI function, legal surface, authority, tier, and clause route.
threat_matrix.proof_line: 98 threat units · 17 fields · Lane A / Lane B routing · IN / EU / US authority mapping
threat_matrix.integrity_note: If a jurisdiction is not mapped, the cell stays blank. The matrix does not invent fake IN/EU/US parallels just to look complete.
threat_matrix.guide_text: Filter the matrix or click a row to see how a threat becomes a document route.

authority.us: Specht v. Netscape Communications Corp. (2d Cir. 2002)
Meyer v. Uber Technologies, Inc. (2d Cir. 2017)
Bragg v. Linden Research, Inc. (E.D. Pa. 2007)
FTC Act §5 / ROSCA (15 U.S.C. §8401 et seq.)
NY General Obligations Law §5-903 (as amended Dec 2025)
FTC Negative Option Rule (16 CFR Part 425, rulemaking restarted Jan 30, 2026)
Soteria Insurance Ltd v. IBM United Kingdom Ltd [2022] EWHC 2084 (TCC)
Moffatt v. Air Canada (B.C. Civil Resolution Tribunal, 2024)

instrument_architecture.title: Instrument Architecture — the document stack is a legal system
instrument_architecture.intro: The documents are not standalone files. Each instrument has a role, dependency, and trigger. The stack works because the documents route into each other.
instrument_architecture.guide_line: Hover a document to see its role. Click to open the stack logic. Try a route to see which instruments activate together.

living_document.title: Living Document — how risk becomes an instrument
living_document.intro: Select a threat or commercial trigger. The viewer reveals the clause, connected documents, and the legal logic that makes the instrument work.
living_document.guide_line: Click a trigger to reveal the clause and connected instruments.

triggers.label: RAG-only / Deletion Architecture
triggers.lane: Lane A, with Lane B relevance where internal AI tools process personal data

triggers.label: AI Output / HITL Authorship
triggers.lane: Both, but main viewer route should start with Lane A ToS and show Lane B support documents

triggers.label: Indemnity Routing
triggers.lane: Lane A, with Lane B flow-down relevance

triggers.label: Data Processing / Subprocessors
triggers.lane: Lane A, with Lane B relevance where employee/internal data is processed

triggers.label: AUP / Prohibited Use
triggers.lane: Lane A, with Lane B policy analogue

triggers.label: Suspension / Termination Route
triggers.lane: Lane A, with cross-document enforcement relevance

vault_router.title: Vault Router — how the stack becomes dynamic
vault_router.intro: The document map is the static architecture. The Vault turns client/product answers into document routing.
vault_router.guide_line: Toggle a product fact to see which instruments activate.

act2.connector2: A document stack is still hand-work if someone must read the registry, select the clauses, and assemble the file manually. The leverage was not the document alone — it was the system that could produce it. So I built that.
act2.connector_visual: Instrument architecture → intake logic → automated assembly

act3.header: Act III — The Funnel: the machine that runs it.
act3.scope_note: Live tools (the four sandboxes) and the videos are a separate build phase. Structure, copy, the OS map, the automation-library link, and Connector 3 are real now.
act3.determinism_principle: Here's why you can trust the funnel: the registry constrains the model, the Vault→Assembly is deterministic, and the residual judgment goes to human review. As deterministic as law requires.

master_video.frame: Primary exhibit — a chaptered full-power walkthrough of the project-version engines (most powerful models, in the ChatGPT/Claude project interface), narrated for the prompt/agent engineering. Chapters: Signal → Diligence → Assembly → CRM. Shows the client-deliverable docs (Architect Notes excluded) and the threat-registry CSV as provenance. CRM chapter uses synthetic data only.

act3.os_map: One synthetic record traced end-to-end through the stations.

stations.name: Signal
stations.frame: Engineering proof, not a product — multi-agent LLM pipeline.

stations.name: Diligence
stations.frame: Run a URL or upload a doc → Risk Report (mechanical trigger → law IN/US/EU → remediation). AI-risk diligence; the same engine generalises to any diligence domain.

stations.name: Assembly
stations.frame: One closed-loop engine, step-by-step, synthetic input. Law-as-code. Manage = synthetic sandbox, never the real portal.

stations.name: Lane B Scanner
stations.frame: The deploy-side counterpart — the key Lane B artifact.

stations.name: Automation Engine
stations.frame: Public, audited repo (real now) + a runnable document pipeline: drop a doc → OCR → extract → classify → summarise → route. The same architecture, generalised.

act3.connector3: Every system here runs on one spine — the scanner reads the registry, the Vault assembles the clauses, and the platform proves the architecture isn't specific to law at all. Which is the whole point.

capstone.title: One Mind: The Legal Problem First — Across Three Jurisdictions

crossborder.in: IT Act 2000 → DPDP Act 2023 · CPA 2019 · Copyright Act 1957 · IT Amendment Rules 2026 (3-hour takedown) · MeitY advisories
crossborder.eu: GDPR · EU AI Act 2024/1689 (Art. 50: 2 Aug 2026; high-risk Annex III: 2 Dec 2027) · Product Liability Directive 2024/2853
crossborder.us: UCC Art. 2/2A · UETA §14 · CCPA/CPRA · TX TRAIGA · IL HB3773 (Colorado AI Act: repealed/replaced — not active)

parallel_map.header: The same legal discipline, even in the engineering.
parallel_map.frame: The law came first and trained the instinct — classification, dependency, controlled assembly — and it shows up when I build software. Not a method that generalises across domains; a legal mind that shaped the code.

capstone.identity_line: A litigator who taught himself to build — not a builder who picked up law. The legal judgment leads; the code is only how I ship it.

close.body: I'm a lawyer by instinct, not by accident. I spent three and a half years in a courtroom before I wrote a line of code, and what drew me next wasn't technology for its own sake — it was the place where law and technology collide. Privacy, data protection, AI: the questions there are still open, the old answers don't quite fit, and that's exactly what pulled me in. So I taught myself to build — not to leave the law, but to go deeper into it than paper alone would let me. The technology made me a sharper lawyer; it was never the other way round. I didn't become a builder who picked up law. I stayed a lawyer who learned to build. I'd rather do this work — at the edge of tech and privacy law — inside a team already taking it seriously.
close.governance_note: Models are routed by data sensitivity. Public-only diagnostics run on free fast-inference tiers — no privacy cost, the input is already public. Client and work data run on paid API tiers with model training disabled. Every demo here uses public or synthetic data only.
close.genuineness: All work shown is mine; every link and demo is genuine.
